summary: Jeff Atwood observes that most users, including experienced software developers, inefficiently navigate login forms by constantly switching between mouse and keyboard. He documents the eight-step mouse-heavy process people commonly use versus the simple four-step keyboard-only alternative using Tab and Enter. The post argues that this basic keyboard efficiency should be common knowledge but surprisingly isn't. Atwood encourages readers to spread awareness of this simple technique to help colleagues save time during their daily login routines.
key_points: Even experienced developers often use inefficient mouse-heavy workflows for simple tasks like logging in
The keyboard-only login method (type, Tab, type, Enter) is dramatically more efficient than mouse navigation
Constant transitions between mouse and keyboard are a hidden productivity drain
Login forms are so ubiquitous that even small efficiency gains compound significantly
Developers have a responsibility to ensure standard keyboard navigation works in their login forms
money_quotes: Every time I watch someone do this, a little part of me dies inside.

I'm not just talking about casual users like our parents. I'm talking about our fellow software developers, and other users who work with the computer for most of the day. People who really should know better.
What kills me about this is all the needless, painful transitions between the mouse and the keyboard.
Your fingers are already on the keyboard while you're typing – just add a little Tab and Enter to the mix!
All I want is to save users a few precious seconds as they slog through the endless logins during their work day.
key_insight: Small inefficiencies in ubiquitous interactions like login forms compound into significant wasted time, and even technically sophisticated users often fail to optimize their most frequent workflows.
